Sub-shelf melt fluxes reach their maximum in 2150. No exact value is provided, but it is implied to be large as ice shelves progressively collapse. Assigning 0 since no specific value given but positive.
Surface runoff reaches median values of almost 6000 gigatons per year by 2300. This contributes to global mean sea-level rise.
This value is for West Antarctica, and represents the rate of mass loss under both atmospheric and oceanic forcings by the end of the millennium.
Increased median sea-level contribution due to hydrofracturing.
Increased median sea-level contribution due to hydrofracturing.
Projected surface runoff fluxes over the Antarctic grounded ice sheet reach median values of approximately 4000 Gt/yr by the year 2300.
By approximately 2140, the Surface Mass Balance (SMB) over grounded ice becomes lower than its present-day value, initially due to an increase in ice discharge.
When near-surface warming exceeds approximately +7.5°C, surface runoff surpasses snow accumulation, leading to a negative median surface mass balance over ice shelves.
Sea-level contribution from the Antarctic ice sheet under SSP1-2.6 scenario by the end of the century shows a range from -8 to 16 cm based on the 5–95% calibrated probability interval.
Sea-level contribution from the Antarctic ice sheet under SSP5-8.5 scenario by the end of the century shows a range from -8 to 16 cm based on the 5–95% calibrated probability interval.
Sea-level contribution from the Antarctic ice sheet under SSP1-2.6 scenario by 2300 shows a range from -0.26 to 1.56 m based on the 5–95% calibrated probability interval.
Sea-level contribution from the Antarctic ice sheet under SSP5-8.5 scenario by 2300 shows a range from 0.46 to 4.52 m based on the 5–95% calibrated probability interval.
